nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater tributaries and streams of the upper Amazon basin, typically under dense forest canopy with dark, tannin-stained water, sandy substrate, leaf litter, and abundant aquatic and riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Amazon swords, Vallisneria, floating plants for shade)
dried leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to release tannins
river sand or fine dark substrate
dim or diffused lighting to bring out gold iridescence

diet.notes: Unfussy and readily accepts most small aquarium foods. Offer a varied diet of high-quality micro-pellets or crushed flakes alongside frozen or live small foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and baby brine shrimp. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, worms, tiny crustaceans, and plant matter. Regular offerings of live or frozen foods improve coloration and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: other small tetras (neon, cardinal, ember, black phantom)
avoidWith: aggressive or fast-moving species that stress shy schoolers

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species photo; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: The Garnet Tetra is a small, peaceful Peruvian Amazon characin reaching ~4.5 cm, distinguished by its gold-iridescent flanks, black wedge marking, and red caudal spot. Less common in trade than other Hemigrammus, it is best kept in groups of 8+ in soft, slightly acidic water with plenty of planting and tannin-stained conditions to display its full coloration.
careNotes:
The Garnet Tetra (Hemigrammus pulcher) is an attractive but somewhat underrepresented species in the hobby, native to slow-moving blackwater tributaries of the upper Peruvian Amazon. Adults reach approximately 4–4.5 cm TL and display a striking gold-iridescent body with a dark wedge mark running into the caudal peduncle and a distinctive red spot at the base of the tail — a coloration pattern that can cause confusion with Bleher’s Rummy-Nose Tetra (Hemigrammus bleheri, fw-fish-183) in trade, though the Garnet lacks the full red head pattern of the rummy-nose group and instead shows the gold-body iridescence as its primary ornament.

Being naturally from soft, acidic blackwater, H. pulcher thrives at pH 5.5–7.0 and gH 1–12. While it can adapt to moderately hard water for general community keeping, the best coloration and long-term health are seen in softer, slightly acidic conditions. A minimum tank of 70 L (60 cm footprint) suits a group of 8–10; larger tanks allow more natural schooling behavior. The tank benefits from a dark or sandy substrate, driftwood, leaf litter, and dense planting. Dim lighting or floating plants to filter surface light help the fish feel secure and show their iridescent gold flanks to best effect.

Keep in groups of at least 8, preferably 10 or more — smaller numbers lead to shyness and washed-out color. This species is compatible with most small, peaceful community fish sharing similar water preferences. Avoid boisterous or significantly larger tankmates.
breedingNotes: Breeding is achievable but moderately challenging compared to hardier tetras. A dedicated breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5) at 26–28°C is recommended. Use peat filtration or aged blackwater, fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop as egg deposition sites, and low lighting. Adults are egg scatterers and must be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg and fry predation. Eggs hatch within 20–36 hours; fry are free-swimming after a further 2–3 days. Start fry on infusoria or commercially prepared micro-fry foods, transitioning to Artemia nauplii after the first week. Conditioning with live and frozen foods before attempting breeding improves spawning success. Males can be identified by a small characin hook on the anal fin.

primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size 4.5 cm, temperature 23–27°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness 1–12°H, minimum tank 60 cm / 70 L, native range (upper Amazon, Peru), diet, breeding parameters (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, temperature 80–84°F for spawning), behavior, and compatibility notes.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity — Spawning the Pretty / Garnet Tetra
additional.notes: Supplementary breeding detail: egg hatching timeline 20–24 hours, fry free-swimming after 2–3 days, males identified by characin hook on anal fin, near-Iquitos Peru locality noted. Confirmed soft acidic breeding water requirements.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Hemigrammus pulcher (Garnet Tetra)
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming adult size ~4.5 cm TL, swim zone (middle water column), minimum 60 cm tank for a group of 6–8, hardness up to 18 dH noted as maximum tolerance. Native range given as Peru, Brazil, possibly Colombia. Breeding cannibalism risk highlighted.

fish.breedingNotes: Requires soft, acidic breeding water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5) at 26–28°C with fine-leaved plants or spawning mop. Egg scatterers; adults must be removed post-spawning to prevent predation. Eggs hatch 20–36 hours; fry free-swim after 2–3 days. Start on infusoria, then Artemia nauplii.
